In November, as a kind of online found-video installation, he decided to create a Web site devoted to collecting the videos.

Riley Walz, a twenty-two-year-old artist and programmer in San Francisco, came across the videos earlier this year thanks to a [blog post](https://ben-mini.com/2024/img-0416) written by the engineer Ben Wallace, and he got hooked on watching them.

One day, a friend listed the address as such on Google Maps and, over time, others added fake glowing reviews. Then they turned the joke into a one-night pop-up steak restaurant, complete with a legitimate temporary alcohol permit, and hosted diners who may not have realized they were getting dinner cooked by a crew of young amateurs.

Walz’s interventions provoke strong reactions because they call attention to the hidden reservoirs of real-time personal data that power our daily lives in the iPhone era, whether we’re requesting driving directions or Googling a news event or looking for a restaurant recommendation.

excerpts: # Dresden Bot
Telegram bot for the DVB and VVO transit network in Dresden, Germany. Try it now at [t.me/dresdenbot](https://t.me/dresdenbot)

The bot uses the DVB mobile website's API to get real-time departures from specific stops, and to use the trip planner to get from one stop to another. The code is really messy and probably isn't readable, I made this quickly and copy and pasted a lot of code, there's duplicate code everywhere... :)

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco’s Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it “Bop Spotter,” after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.

In 2020, he created a Twitter account for a fake Republican congressional candidate named Andrew Walz and—with the help of an A.I.-generated portrait, a fake Web site, and a fake listing on the candidate directory Ballotpedia—managed to get the account verified with a blue check.
Titled “ [IMG_0001](https://walzr.com/IMG_0001) ,” it is formatted to look like a television screen, with an image of a remote that you click to turn the footage on.
In November, as a kind of online found-video installation, he decided to create a Web site devoted to collecting the videos.

Riley Walz, a twenty-two-year-old artist and programmer in San Francisco, came across the videos earlier this year thanks to a [blog post](https://ben-mini.com/2024/img-0416) written by the engineer Ben Wallace, and he got hooked on watching them.

One day, a friend listed the address as such on Google Maps and, over time, others added fake glowing reviews. Then they turned the joke into a one-night pop-up steak restaurant, complete with a legitimate temporary alcohol permit, and hosted diners who may not have realized they were getting dinner cooked by a crew of young amateurs.

Walz’s interventions provoke strong reactions because they call attention to the hidden reservoirs of real-time personal data that power our daily lives in the iPhone era, whether we’re requesting driving directions or Googling a news event or looking for a restaurant recommendation.

Where you are, what you’re looking at, and what you like is being tracked more or less constantly—either you’re doing it to yourself voluntarily, by taking a video with your phone and posting it online, say, or a corporate entity is doing it for you.
citations.title: The Artist Exposing the Data We Leave Online | The New Yorker

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco’s Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it “Bop Spotter,” after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.

In 2020, he created a Twitter account for a fake Republican congressional candidate named Andrew Walz and—with the help of an A.I.-generated portrait, a fake Web site, and a fake listing on the candidate directory Ballotpedia—managed to get the account verified with a blue check.
Titled “ [IMG_0001](https://walzr.com/IMG_0001) ,” it is formatted to look like a television screen, with an image of a remote that you click to turn the footage on.
In November, as a kind of online found-video installation, he decided to create a Web site devoted to collecting the videos.

Riley Walz, a twenty-two-year-old artist and programmer in San Francisco, came across the videos earlier this year thanks to a [blog post](https://ben-mini.com/2024/img-0416) written by the engineer Ben Wallace, and he got hooked on watching them.

One day, a friend listed the address as such on Google Maps and, over time, others added fake glowing reviews. Then they turned the joke into a one-night pop-up steak restaurant, complete with a legitimate temporary alcohol permit, and hosted diners who may not have realized they were getting dinner cooked by a crew of young amateurs.

Walz’s interventions provoke strong reactions because they call attention to the hidden reservoirs of real-time personal data that power our daily lives in the iPhone era, whether we’re requesting driving directions or Googling a news event or looking for a restaurant recommendation.

Where you are, what you’re looking at, and what you like is being tracked more or less constantly—either you’re doing it to yourself voluntarily, by taking a video with your phone and posting it online, say, or a corporate entity is doing it for you.
citations.title: The Artist Exposing the Data We Leave Online | The New Yorker

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco’s Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it “Bop Spotter,” after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.

Walz’s interventions provoke strong reactions because they call attention to the hidden reservoirs of real-time personal data that power our daily lives in the iPhone era, whether we’re requesting driving directions or Googling a news event or looking for a restaurant recommendation.

Where you are, what you’re looking at, and what you like is being tracked more or less constantly—either you’re doing it to yourself voluntarily, by taking a video with your phone and posting it online, say, or a corporate entity is doing it for you.

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco’s Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it “Bop Spotter,” after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.

In 2020, he created a Twitter account for a fake Republican congressional candidate named Andrew Walz and—with the help of an A.I.-generated portrait, a fake Web site, and a fake listing on the candidate directory Ballotpedia—managed to get the account verified with a blue check.
Titled “ [IMG_0001](https://walzr.com/IMG_0001) ,” it is formatted to look like a television screen, with an image of a remote that you click to turn the footage on.
In November, as a kind of online found-video installation, he decided to create a Web site devoted to collecting the videos.

Riley Walz, a twenty-two-year-old artist and programmer in San Francisco, came across the videos earlier this year thanks to a [blog post](https://ben-mini.com/2024/img-0416) written by the engineer Ben Wallace, and he got hooked on watching them.

One day, a friend listed the address as such on Google Maps and, over time, others added fake glowing reviews. Then they turned the joke into a one-night pop-up steak restaurant, complete with a legitimate temporary alcohol permit, and hosted diners who may not have realized they were getting dinner cooked by a crew of young amateurs.

Walz’s interventions provoke strong reactions because they call attention to the hidden reservoirs of real-time personal data that power our daily lives in the iPhone era, whether we’re requesting driving directions or Googling a news event or looking for a restaurant recommendation.

Where you are, what you’re looking at, and what you like is being tracked more or less constantly—either you’re doing it to yourself voluntarily, by taking a video with your phone and posting it online, say, or a corporate entity is doing it for you.
citations.title: The Artist Exposing the Data We Leave Online | The New Yorker

excerpts: Launched in 2024, _IMG_0001_ cycles through five million YouTube clips that were uploaded directly from early iPhone models with default filenames (e.g., IMG_0001).

On September 23, 2025, Walz launched _Find My Parking Cops_ , a map that deduced the near real-time locations and routes of San Francisco parking-enforcement officers by scraping predictable citation IDs from the city's online payment portal.

Walz's work blends programming and culture reporting instincts into small, public-facing experiments. The New Yorker describes his work as existing within "a lineage of prankster art that used the Internet both as a medium and as a venue".
Riley Walz is an American software engineer and internet artist.

citations.title: Riley Walz - Wikipedia

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco’s Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it “Bop Spotter,” after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.

In 2020, he created a Twitter account for a fake Republican congressional candidate named Andrew Walz and—with the help of an A.I.-generated portrait, a fake Web site, and a fake listing on the candidate directory Ballotpedia—managed to get the account verified with a blue check.
Titled “ [IMG_0001](https://walzr.com/IMG_0001) ,” it is formatted to look like a television screen, with an image of a remote that you click to turn the footage on.
In November, as a kind of online found-video installation, he decided to create a Web site devoted to collecting the videos.

Riley Walz, a twenty-two-year-old artist and programmer in San Francisco, came across the videos earlier this year thanks to a [blog post](https://ben-mini.com/2024/img-0416) written by the engineer Ben Wallace, and he got hooked on watching them.

One day, a friend listed the address as such on Google Maps and, over time, others added fake glowing reviews. Then they turned the joke into a one-night pop-up steak restaurant, complete with a legitimate temporary alcohol permit, and hosted diners who may not have realized they were getting dinner cooked by a crew of young amateurs.

Walz’s interventions provoke strong reactions because they call attention to the hidden reservoirs of real-time personal data that power our daily lives in the iPhone era, whether we’re requesting driving directions or Googling a news event or looking for a restaurant recommendation.

Where you are, what you’re looking at, and what you like is being tracked more or less constantly—either you’re doing it to yourself voluntarily, by taking a video with your phone and posting it online, say, or a corporate entity is doing it for you.
citations.title: The Artist Exposing the Data We Leave Online | The New Yorker

excerpts: In 2020, he created a Twitter account for a fake Republican congressional candidate named Andrew Walz and—with the help of an A.I.-generated portrait, a fake Web site, and a fake listing on the candidate directory Ballotpedia—managed to get the account verified with a blue check.

Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco’s Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it “Bop Spotter,” after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.
Titled “ [IMG_0001](https://walzr.com/IMG_0001) ,” it is formatted to look like a television screen, with an image of a remote that you click to turn the footage on.
In November, as a kind of online found-video installation, he decided to create a Web site devoted to collecting the videos.

Riley Walz, a twenty-two-year-old artist and programmer in San Francisco, came across the videos earlier this year thanks to a [blog post](https://ben-mini.com/2024/img-0416) written by the engineer Ben Wallace, and he got hooked on watching them.

One day, a friend listed the address as such on Google Maps and, over time, others added fake glowing reviews. Then they turned the joke into a one-night pop-up steak restaurant, complete with a legitimate temporary alcohol permit, and hosted diners who may not have realized they were getting dinner cooked by a crew of young amateurs.

Walz’s interventions provoke strong reactions because they call attention to the hidden reservoirs of real-time personal data that power our daily lives in the iPhone era, whether we’re requesting driving directions or Googling a news event or looking for a restaurant recommendation.

Where you are, what you’re looking at, and what you like is being tracked more or less constantly—either you’re doing it to yourself voluntarily, by taking a video with your phone and posting it online, say, or a corporate entity is doing it for you.
citations.title: The Artist Exposing the Data We Leave Online | The New Yorker

excerpts: There were some technical challenges because it's not actually using the Shazam app. It's like this old Android phone that runs a script where it's in airplane mode for most of the time, but then every 10 minutes it will connect to nearby public Wi-Fi and then upload the last 10 minutes of audio to my server. Then on my server, it will chop the 10-minute audio file into 10-second clips, and then pass each clip to Shazam’s API, and then if it finds a match, it adds it to the database, which adds to the site.

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco’s Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it “Bop Spotter,” after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.

Walz’s interventions provoke strong reactions because they call attention to the hidden reservoirs of real-time personal data that power our daily lives in the iPhone era, whether we’re requesting driving directions or Googling a news event or looking for a restaurant recommendation.

Where you are, what you’re looking at, and what you like is being tracked more or less constantly—either you’re doing it to yourself voluntarily, by taking a video with your phone and posting it online, say, or a corporate entity is doing it for you.
citations.title: The Artist Exposing the Data We Leave Online | The New Yorker

excerpts: I use Shazam all the time. When I hear a song out in person and listen to it later I feel the way I felt when I first heard it. I remember who I was with and where I was, and I just like it a lot.

There were some technical challenges because it's not actually using the Shazam app. It's like this old Android phone that runs a script where it's in airplane mode for most of the time, but then every 10 minutes it will connect to nearby public Wi-Fi and then upload the last 10 minutes of audio to my server. Then on my server, it will chop the 10-minute audio file into 10-second clips, and then pass each clip to Shazam’s API, and then if it finds a match, it adds it to the database, which adds to the site.
citations.title: The Guy Behind the Most Nostalgic Sites on the Internet - WIRED

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco’s Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it “Bop Spotter,” after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco’s Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it “Bop Spotter,” after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.

Riley Walz, a twenty-two-year-old artist and programmer in San Francisco, came across the videos earlier this year thanks to a [blog post](https://ben-mini.com/2024/img-0416) written by the engineer Ben Wallace, and he got hooked on watching them.

Walz’s interventions provoke strong reactions because they call attention to the hidden reservoirs of real-time personal data that power our daily lives in the iPhone era, whether we’re requesting driving directions or Googling a news event or looking for a restaurant recommendation.

Where you are, what you’re looking at, and what you like is being tracked more or less constantly—either you’re doing it to yourself voluntarily, by taking a video with your phone and posting it online, say, or a corporate entity is doing it for you.

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco’s Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it “Bop Spotter,” after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.

Riley Walz, a twenty-two-year-old artist and programmer in San Francisco, came across the videos earlier this year thanks to a [blog post](https://ben-mini.com/2024/img-0416) written by the engineer Ben Wallace, and he got hooked on watching them.

In 2020, he created a Twitter account for a fake Republican congressional candidate named Andrew Walz and—with the help of an A.I.-generated portrait, a fake Web site, and a fake listing on the candidate directory Ballotpedia—managed to get the account verified with a blue check.
In November, as a kind of online found-video installation, he decided to create a Web site devoted to collecting the videos.

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco’s Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it “Bop Spotter,” after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.

In 2020, he created a Twitter account for a fake Republican congressional candidate named Andrew Walz and—with the help of an A.I.-generated portrait, a fake Web site, and a fake listing on the candidate directory Ballotpedia—managed to get the account verified with a blue check.

In November, as a kind of online found-video installation, he decided to create a Web site devoted to collecting the videos.

Riley Walz, a twenty-two-year-old artist and programmer in San Francisco, came across the videos earlier this year thanks to a [blog post](https://ben-mini.com/2024/img-0416) written by the engineer Ben Wallace, and he got hooked on watching them.

One day, a friend listed the address as such on Google Maps and, over time, others added fake glowing reviews. Then they turned the joke into a one-night pop-up steak restaurant, complete with a legitimate temporary alcohol permit, and hosted diners who may not have realized they were getting dinner cooked by a crew of young amateurs.

Walz’s interventions provoke strong reactions because they call attention to the hidden reservoirs of real-time personal data that power our daily lives in the iPhone era, whether we’re requesting driving directions or Googling a news event or looking for a restaurant recommendation.

Where you are, what you’re looking at, and what you like is being tracked more or less constantly—either you’re doing it to yourself voluntarily, by taking a video with your phone and posting it online, say, or a corporate entity is doing it for you.

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco’s Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it “Bop Spotter,” after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.

content.executive_summary: Riley Walz is a San Francisco-based creative software engineer and web-creative recognized for a series of playful, technically ingenious projects and public stunts that explore the intersection of technology, culture, and social systems. His work, primarily documented on his portfolio site walzr.com and announced via his X handle @rtwlz, consistently garners significant media attention and public engagement. Key projects include 'Bop Spotter,' a public installation using a solar-powered phone to Shazam music on the street; 'Routeshuffle,' a random route generator for runners; and 'IMG_0001,' a website that surfaces millions of unedited, default-named videos from YouTube, evoking a sense of digital nostalgia. Walz is also known for high-profile stunts such as creating a fake NYC steakhouse ('Mehran's Steak House') that evolved from a Google Maps prank into a real one-night pop-up event, orchestrating a 'mass-hail' of Waymo vehicles to create a self-driving traffic jam, and getting a fake 2020 political candidate verified by Twitter. The comprehensive source map expansion reveals a consistent creative process characterized by rapid prototyping, data scraping, reverse-engineering public systems (like SFMTA's parking ticket data), and clever API usage. His thinking style is driven by personal curiosity, a taste for nostalgia, and a desire to playfully subvert platforms and expose hidden data, often framing his work as 'culture surveillance' or a 'mirror to our collective vanity.' His projects have had a tangible impact, prompting responses from major platforms like Twitter, Google, and Waymo, and leading to professional recognition, including a reported hire by OpenAI.
content.identity_verification_summary: The identity of Riley Walz as the creative software engineer behind the specified projects is established with high confidence through a robust network of cross-referencing evidence. The canonical hub for his identity is his personal portfolio, walzr.com, which lists his projects and often contains first-person write-ups. This site is directly linked from his verified X (formerly Twitter) account, @rtwlz, which he uses to announce projects and provide commentary. For instance, his X profile bio links to walzr.com, and his tweets about the San Francisco parking ticket project and IMG_0001 link directly to the corresponding pages on his portfolio. Furthermore, his primary GitHub account, 'rtwalz', lists walzr.com in its profile, and its repositories (e.g., 'musical-roulette') correspond to projects hosted on subdomains of his site. Independent project sites like routeshuffle.com also contain self-identifying text, such as, 'I'm Riley, a distance runner from New York. I made Routeshuffle...'. This web of first-party connections is strongly corroborated by numerous reputable press outlets. Articles in WIRED, The New York Times, NPR, KQED, and SFGATE consistently name Riley Walz as the creator of projects like Bop Spotter, the fake steakhouse, and the parking ticket app, and these articles frequently cite his @rtwlz X handle and link to walzr.com. This triangulation between his self-owned platforms and independent journalistic verification confirms that the individual associated with walzr.com, @rtwlz, and the GitHub handle 'rtwalz' is the same person responsible for all the analyzed projects. Research explicitly excluded other individuals named 'Riley Walz' found on professional networking sites who had no discernible link to these canonical projects or profiles.
content.reliability_tier_framework:
To assess the credibility and nature of each source, a three-tiered reliability framework was utilized, prioritizing sources based on their proximity to the creator and their editorial rigor.

**Tier 1: Primary & First-Party Sources**
*   **Definition:** This tier includes materials authored or directly controlled by Riley Walz. These are the most authoritative sources for understanding his intent, self-described methods, and for obtaining original project artifacts.
*   **Examples:** His personal portfolio (walzr.com) and its project subpages (e.g., walzr.com/bop-spotter), independent project microsites (routeshuffle.com), his primary GitHub repositories (e.g., github.com/rtwalz), and downloadable data artifacts (like the Bop Spotter CSV).
*   **Justification:** These sources provide direct, unmediated information from the creator. They are canonical for project descriptions, technical caveats, and authorial statements. Archived copies of these pages (via Wayback Machine) inherit this tier but are noted as archival.

**Tier 2: Major Press & Reputable Institutional Coverage**
*   **Definition:** This tier comprises in-depth profiles, interviews, and reports from established and reputable media outlets, including major national publications, local public media, and respected tech/culture journals.
*   **Examples:** Feature articles and interviews in WIRED, The New York Times, NPR, KQED Arts, SFGATE, and CNN. These sources often contain direct quotes from Walz, detailed technical descriptions gathered from interviews, and contextual analysis.
*   **Justification:** These sources are subject to editorial oversight and fact-checking, lending them high credibility. They provide independent corroboration of Walz's claims and offer valuable third-party perspectives on the impact and context of his work.

**Tier 3: Secondary Mentions, Aggregators, and Social Chatter**
*   **Definition:** This tier includes sources that summarize, reference, or discuss Walz's work without direct interviews or original reporting. It also includes social media posts from the general public and unverified accounts.
*   **Examples:** Syndicated news articles (e.g., Yahoo Tech reprinting other stories), user-edited summaries (like Wikipedia), community discussion threads (e.g., Hacker News), and social media posts from individuals reacting to his projects.
*   **Justification:** While useful for gauging public reaction and discovering related leads, these sources lack the authority of Tier 1 and the editorial rigor of Tier 2. Information from this tier is used for supplementary context and is always cross-checked with higher-tier sources before being considered factual.

key_projects_overview.description: A public art installation and data project in San Francisco's Mission District. It consists of a microphone that continuously listens to street-level sounds, uses the Shazam service to identify music being played, and posts the results to a live feed on its website. The project is framed by its creator as a form of 'culture surveillance' aimed at 'catching vibes' rather than criminals.
key_projects_overview.methods_and_technologies: The system is built using an old, solar-powered Android phone housed in a plastic box. The phone runs a custom script that records audio and, every 10 minutes, connects to public Wi-Fi to upload the audio to a server. The server-side script then segments the audio into 10-second clips and feeds them to the Shazam API for identification. Matched songs are added to a database and displayed on the website. A full CSV download of all detected songs is made available to the public.
key_projects_overview.key_insights_into_process: Walz's motivation was a blend of playful subversion and genuine curiosity. He stated, 'I just thought it was kind of a funny little joke to pick up songs instead of gunshots.' He also described his personal habit that inspired the project: 'I use Shazam all the time... So I thought it would be cool to Shazam from one fixed place and just see the results over time.' He envisions expanding it into a global network to 'compare the musical findings between cities.'

key_projects_overview.description: A short-lived web tool that displayed the near real-time locations of San Francisco parking enforcement officers. The site allowed users to see where each officer had last issued a ticket, effectively creating a map to avoid parking enforcement.
key_projects_overview.methods_and_technologies: The project was created by reverse-engineering the San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency's (SFMTA) parking ticket system data feed. By accessing this data, Walz was able to track ticket issuance events and map them to specific officers and locations.
key_projects_overview.key_insights_into_process: This project exemplifies Walz's interest in making opaque public data accessible and creating tools with a rebellious, utilitarian purpose. His announcement tweet captured this spirit: 'I reverse engineered the San Francisco parking ticket system... So I made a website. Find My Friends? AVOID THE PARKING COPS.' The project's ephemerality—it was shut down by the SFMTA within four hours—highlights the risks and realities of relying on unofficial data sources.

key_projects_overview.description: A web project that serves as an archive of approximately 5 million YouTube videos that have default camera filenames, such as 'IMG_0001.mov'. The site presents these videos randomly, framing them as 'unedited, pure moments from random lives' and a time capsule of early, uncurated digital life.
key_projects_overview.methods_and_technologies: A bot was created to crawl YouTube, using its API to search for videos with default filenames. The website is a lightweight, single-serving site that embeds the discovered YouTube videos for random playback. The entire project was a rapid prototype developed over a weekend.
key_projects_overview.key_insights_into_process: This project showcases Walz's rapid prototyping style and his nostalgia for the 'old internet'. He stated he threw the site together in 'four or five hours total' to test the public's reaction. His announcement highlights the concept: 'i found millions of YouTube videos that have default camera names as titles... and made it into a website where you can watch random ones.'

key_projects_overview.description: A simple, single-purpose web utility that generates random routes for running, walking, or cycling. Users input a starting location and a desired distance, and the tool creates a loop route for them.
key_projects_overview.methods_and_technologies: A web application that integrates with a mapping service (like OpenStreetMap or Google Maps) to generate and display routes.
key_projects_overview.key_insights_into_process: This is one of Walz's earlier projects, born from a personal need. On the site's 'About' page, he writes: 'I'm Riley, a distance runner from New York. I made Routeshuffle to help with my summer training in high school.' This demonstrates his long-standing practice of building practical tools to solve his own problems.

key_projects_overview.description: A data visualization project that displays the inferred number of passengers on Amtrak trains across the United States in near real-time.
key_projects_overview.methods_and_technologies: The passenger counts are not direct but are inferred by scraping and analyzing data from Amtrak's public reservation inventory system. The visualization is displayed on a map using data from Protomaps and OpenStreetMap.
key_projects_overview.key_insights_into_process: Walz is transparent about the project's methodology and its limitations, noting on the site: 'Counts are inferred from reservation inventory and may undercount unreserved tickets, pass riders, or routes where Amtrak does not expose full seat inventory.' This highlights his expertise in data scraping and his commitment to providing context for inferred data.

key_projects_overview.description: A project that claims to have scraped the public Spotify and Pandora playlists of celebrities, politicians, and journalists to reveal their listening habits over time.
key_projects_overview.methods_and_technologies: The project page states that it involved finding the real social music accounts of public figures and scraping their playlists and listening activity for over a year. This includes continuously scraping songs they played, play counts, and timestamps.
key_projects_overview.key_insights_into_process: This project aligns with Walz's recurring theme of playful 'surveillance' and exposing hidden data about public life. His first-person description on the site is direct: 'I found the real Spotify accounts... I’ve been scraping their playlists for over a year... I know what songs they played, how many times, and when.'

key_projects_overview.description: A provocative data art project that maps restaurants in a city based on the algorithmically determined 'attractiveness' of the people who review them online.
key_projects_overview.methods_and_technologies: The project involved scraping millions of Google Maps restaurant reviews. The profile pictures of the reviewers were then fed into an AI model that rated the person's 'hotness' on a scale of 1 to 10. The average score for each restaurant's reviewers was then calculated and mapped.
key_projects_overview.key_insights_into_process: Walz uses this project to comment on societal biases and the flaws of AI. He explicitly states, 'The model is certainly biased. It's certainly flawed... This website just puts reductive numbers on the superficial calculations we make every day. A mirror held up to our collective vanity.' This shows his use of technology to create social commentary.

key_projects_overview.description: A live feed that monitors and displays the books being checked out in real-time from the Seward Park branch of the New York Public Library (NYPL).
key_projects_overview.methods_and_technologies: The project works by continuously scraping the book availability data from the library's public website. When the system detects that the number of available copies for a specific book has decreased, it registers this event as a checkout and posts the book's title to the feed.
key_projects_overview.key_insights_into_process: Continuing his theme of making public data visible, Walz describes the project as 'a live, unfiltered look at what books are being checked out.' It's another example of his 'surveillance' projects, this time focused on the cultural consumption patterns of a local community institution.

public_stunts_analysis.timeline: The stunt began in April 2022 when a fake Google Maps listing was created for a townhouse. Over the next year and a half, a waitlist for the non-existent restaurant grew to approximately 900 parties. The stunt culminated in a one-night-only pop-up event on September 23, 2023, where about 140 guests were served.
public_stunts_analysis.collaborators: The event was a collaboration between Riley Walz, Mehran Jalali, and Danielle Egan, who later formed a collective called 'Meridian'. Jalali was the public face, Egan served as the creative director for menu and design, and Walz handled logistics, legal filings, and built the reservation website.
public_stunts_analysis.tools_and_logistics: The stunt's foundation was a fake Google Maps/Business listing, amplified by user reviews. Walz built a custom reservation website (mehranssteakhouse.com) to manage the waitlist. For the physical event, Walz handled the logistics of renting a venue and legally operating by obtaining an LLC and a temporary liquor permit. The event was staffed by approximately 60 volunteers.
public_stunts_analysis.public_and_platform_response: The public and media reaction was largely positive and amused, with coverage in SFGATE and The New York Times. Guests who attended were reportedly delighted by the 'insane, bizarre, but delightful' experience. After the stunt gained press attention, Google removed the fake business listing from its platform.

public_stunts_analysis.timeline: During the 2019-2020 election cycle, Walz, then a 17-year-old student, created a fake congressional candidate named 'Andrew Walz' to test Twitter's new candidate verification policy. The account was successfully verified by Twitter before being reported on by CNN and subsequently suspended in February 2020.
public_stunts_analysis.collaborators: This was a solo project undertaken by Riley Walz.
public_stunts_analysis.tools_and_logistics: The execution was remarkably simple and fast. Walz created a basic website in about 20 minutes and a Twitter account in five minutes. The candidate's profile picture was an AI-generated image from the website 'This Person Does Not Exist'. The key to success was exploiting the verification pipeline: he listed the fake candidate on Ballotpedia, which Twitter was using as a source of truth for verifying candidates, without distinguishing between declared and officially filed candidates.
public_stunts_analysis.public_and_platform_response: After being contacted by CNN, Twitter permanently suspended the fake account, stating it violated their rules. Ballotpedia acknowledged its role in the error and updated its procedures to more clearly differentiate candidate statuses. The incident served as a public demonstration of vulnerabilities in platform verification processes and the ease of creating seemingly legitimate political identities online.

public_stunts_analysis.timeline: The event was executed in July 2025. It gained public attention in October 2025 after Riley Walz posted about it on his social media accounts.
public_stunts_analysis.collaborators: Riley Walz organized the event, which involved the participation of approximately 50 individuals.
public_stunts_analysis.tools_and_logistics: The stunt was coordinated using the Waymo app. The 50 participants simultaneously hailed autonomous Waymo vehicles to a single location. They did not get into the cars, instead allowing the vehicles to wait for the pickup window to expire and depart, incurring small no-show fees (around $5 each). Walz conceptually framed the event as a physical 'distributed denial-of-service' (DDOS) attack.
public_stunts_analysis.public_and_platform_response: Waymo's systems reportedly detected the unusual activity and automatically limited the ability to hail rides to that specific two-block radius overnight. A Waymo spokesperson stated that their systems are designed to manage such scenarios, which can also occur organically after large events. The media, including Road & Track and Yahoo Tech, framed the stunt as a mostly harmless prank that successfully stress-tested and revealed an edge case in the autonomous ride-sharing network.

The site itself reflects his minimalist aesthetic.

press_and_interview_coverage.key_quotes_on_process: On motivation: 'It never really started as that. Each project is something that I'm like, ‘Oh yeah, it'd be cool to just do that.’ Even if no one looked at this website, it would still be worth it for me to make for myself.' On Bop Spotter's tech: 'It's like this old Android phone that runs a script where it's in airplane mode for most of the time, but then every 10 minutes it will connect to nearby public Wi‑Fi and then upload the last 10 minutes of audio to my server. Then on my server, it will chop the 10‑minute audio file into 10‑second clips, and then pass each clip to Shazam’s API...'

press_and_interview_coverage.article_title: ‘Bop Spotter’ Is a Secret Phone Eavesdropping on the Mission’s Music Tastes
press_and_interview_coverage.author: Rae Alexandra
press_and_interview_coverage.key_quotes_on_process: On playful subversion: 'I just thought it was kind of a funny little joke to pick up songs instead of gunshots.' On his self-perception: 'I’m just a normal guy who knows something about technology and likes seeing amusing things. I had an idea for Bop Spotter and just wanted to see what would happen.' On inspiration: 'I use Shazam all the time... So I thought it would be cool to Shazam from one fixed place and just see the results over time.'

On his creative habit: 'I have a very long list of things I want to make... And every weekend, I like look at it and take one thing off.' The article also notes the system logs about 150 tracks a day and cost about $100 to build.

The repository shows a complete Flutter project structure, indicating how Walz's web-based concept was translated into a cross-platform mobile application.

social_media_presence.representative_posts_with_insights:
The @rtwlz account is a primary channel for project announcements and brief, first-person insights into his methods. His posts often go viral and are cited by press. Notable examples include:
1. **Find My Parking Cops Announcement (Sep 23, 2025):** 'I reverse engineered the San Francisco parking ticket system. I can see every ticket seconds after it's written So I made a website. Find My Friends? AVOID THE PARKING COPS.' This post (cited with permalink status/2051331552135860347) reveals his method of reverse-engineering a public data system to create a utility with a rebellious tone.
2. **IMG_0001 Announcement (Nov 18, 2024):** 'i found millions of YouTube videos that have default camera names as titles (like IMG_0276) and made it into a website where you can watch random ones. unedited, pure moments from random lives http://walzr.com/IMG_0001'. This post explains the project's core concept: large-scale crawling of YouTube based on a simple heuristic (default filenames) to create a 'time capsule'.

The GitHub profile is a key part of Walz's professional and technical identity. The profile's bio contains a direct link to his canonical portfolio, walzr.com, verifying the connection. While the profile itself is not a traditional social feed, it serves as a public-facing collection of his technical work. The 'posts' are the repositories themselves, which contain code, README files, and commit histories that provide deep, durable insights into his technical methods, preferred programming languages (primarily JavaScript), and development practices. It is the primary source for his technical artifacts.

archival_sources_and_institutional_menti.contextual_notes: Archival captures of Riley Walz's main portfolio site, walzr.com, document the evolution of his projects. The site acts as the canonical hub, providing descriptions and links to all his major works. The archives show his project list growing over time, establishing a timeline of his creative output.

Archival captures of Routeshuffle, one of Walz's earliest public projects. The site's 'About' text contains a direct authorial statement: 'I'm Riley, a distance runner from New York. I made Routeshuffle to help with my summer training in high school.' This provides important context on the project's origin and his long-standing practice of building personal, utility-focused web tools.

The method involved a quickly made website, an AI-generated profile picture from 'This Person Does Not Exist,' and exploiting Twitter's verification pipeline via Ballotpedia. This source establishes an early pattern of probing and exposing vulnerabilities in digital platforms.

The individual project pages on walzr.com serve as primary archival documents. Pages for projects like 'Bop Spotter,' 'Library Spy,' 'LooksMapping,' and 'Find My Parking Cops' contain explicit, first-person descriptions of the methodology, data sources (Shazam, NYPL website, Google Maps), and technical implementation (scraping, reverse-engineering, AI models). They are the canonical source for his own framing of his work, such as describing Bop Spotter as 'culture surveillance'.

content.synthesis_of_creative_process_and_method:
Riley Walz's creative process is defined by agility, resourcefulness, and a multi-disciplinary technical skillset. His methods consistently revolve around a few core practices:

*   **Rapid Prototyping and Iteration:** Walz operates with a 'ship fast' mentality, often developing and launching projects over a single weekend. He described his 'IMG_0001' project to WIRED as being thrown together in 'four or five hours total' to quickly test a concept and gauge public reaction. He maintains a personal backlog of ideas and executes them iteratively, prioritizing curiosity and experimentation over prolonged development cycles.

*   **Data Scraping and Reverse Engineering:** A recurring method is the extraction and visualization of publicly available but often obscured data. This is evident in:
    *   **Find My Parking Cops:** He reverse-engineered the San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency's (SFMTA) parking ticket system to create a real-time map of enforcement officers' locations. The project was so effective that the SFMTA cut off the data source within four hours of its launch.
    *   **Library Spy:** This project continuously scrapes the NYPL website to detect when books are checked out in real-time.
    *   **Amtrak Right Now:** He infers passenger counts on trains by analyzing reservation inventory data.
    *   **LooksMapping:** This involved scraping millions of Google Maps reviews and their associated profile pictures.

*   **Hardware Integration and IoT:** His 'Bop Spotter' project is a prime example of his ability to create physical installations. He built a self-sustaining device using an old Android phone, a solar panel, and a microphone, which connects to public Wi-Fi to upload audio for Shazam identification. This demonstrates a hands-on approach to creating data-gathering systems in the physical world.

*   **API Utilization:** Walz skillfully leverages third-party APIs as core components of his projects. 'Bop Spotter' relies on the Shazam API, 'IMG_0001' used the YouTube API to find videos, and his transit-related projects often interface with transportation data APIs.

*   **Full-Stack Web Development:** His GitHub repositories and project sites indicate proficiency in a range of technologies. He uses JavaScript (likely Node.js) for backend logic, as seen in projects like 'musical-roulette' which also uses the Pug templating engine. He has also created projects or adaptations using mobile frameworks like Flutter and Swift, demonstrating versatility across web and mobile platforms.

*   **Social Engineering and Platform Manipulation:** For his public stunts, his methods extend into the social and logistical realms. The 'Mehran's Steak House' prank involved creating a fake Google Business listing, building a custom reservation website, and obtaining legitimate city permits for a pop-up. The Waymo stunt involved coordinating 50 individuals via the Waymo app to simulate a 'distributed denial-of-service' attack in the physical world.
content.synthesis_of_thinking_style_and_tastes:
Riley Walz's thinking style and aesthetic tastes are characterized by a blend of intellectual curiosity, playful subversion, and a deep appreciation for the hidden narratives within digital systems. Several recurring themes define his work:

*   **Nostalgia and Digital Archaeology:** Walz has a pronounced taste for the aesthetics and artifacts of the early internet. A WIRED profile titled him 'The Guy Behind the Most Nostalgic Sites on the Internet.' His project 'IMG_0001,' which unearths millions of raw, default-named videos from the early days of camera phones, is a direct expression of this. He seeks to create 'living time capsules' that capture 'unedited, pure moments from random lives,' acting as a digital archaeologist of the recent past.

*   **Playful Subversion and Pranksterism:** A significant part of his work involves playfully testing the boundaries of social and technical systems. He described 'Bop Spotter' to KQED as 'a funny little joke to pick up songs instead of gunshots.' His more elaborate stunts, like the fake 2020 candidate that received Twitter verification or the fake steakhouse that duped hundreds, are acts of creative mischief designed to expose the absurdities and vulnerabilities of platforms we take for granted. This prankster ethos is not malicious but rather a method of inquiry and commentary.

*   **Exploration of Public Data and 'Culture Surveillance':** Walz is driven by a fascination with making invisible data visible. Projects like 'Find My Parking Cops,' 'Amtrak Right Now,' and 'Library Spy' all function by revealing data that is technically public but practically inaccessible. He provocatively labels projects like 'Bop Spotter' as 'culture surveillance,' reframing a potentially sinister concept into a tool for 'catching vibes' rather than criminals. This demonstrates a nuanced interest in the ethics and aesthetics of public data.

*   **Intrinsic Motivation and Curiosity:** Across multiple interviews, Walz emphasizes that his primary motivation is personal curiosity. He told WIRED, 'Each project is something that I'm like, ‘Oh yeah, it'd be cool to just do that.’ Even if no one looked at this website, it would still be worth it for me to make for myself.' This intrinsic drive allows him to pursue whimsical or niche ideas without being constrained by commercial viability, positioning his work at the intersection of art, hobby, and engineering.

*   **Simplicity and Utility:** Alongside his more conceptual work, projects like 'Routeshuffle'—a simple, effective tool born from his own needs as a runner—show a taste for clean, utilitarian design that solves a straightforward problem. This highlights a duality in his style: a capacity for both absurd, artistic statements and practical, user-focused products.
content.summary_of_public_and_platform_impact:
Riley Walz's projects and stunts have generated significant public engagement, media commentary, and direct responses from the institutions and platforms they interact with.

**Public and Media Impact:**
His work frequently goes viral, attracting widespread public attention and media coverage. 'Bop Spotter' received hundreds of thousands of visitors in its first week and was featured in major outlets like NPR and WIRED. The 'Mehran's Steak House' stunt attracted a waitlist of 900 parties and was covered by SFGATE and The New York Times as a humorous commentary on exclusivity and hype culture. The media generally portrays his work as a clever and playful form of tech criticism, highlighting his ability to expose vulnerabilities in a non-malicious way. His projects are often framed as whimsical experiments that spark conversation about technology's role in society.

**Platform and Institutional Responses:**
Walz's work has directly prompted reactions and policy changes from several major organizations:
*   **Twitter (now X):** After Walz created a fake 2020 political candidate ('Andrew Walz') and successfully got the account verified, Twitter suspended the account upon being notified by CNN. The incident exposed a significant loophole in their candidate verification process during an election cycle.
*   **Google:** The fake 'Mehran's Steak House' listing on Google Maps was removed by Google after the prank gained widespread press attention, demonstrating a direct platform intervention in response to his stunt.
*   **Ballotpedia:** The fake candidate stunt also revealed that Ballotpedia's system, which Twitter used for verification, did not distinguish between declared and officially filed candidates. The organization subsequently committed to updating its data practices.
*   **San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency (SFMTA):** His 'Find My Parking Cops' website, which provided real-time locations of parking enforcement officers, was shut down just four hours after launch when the SFMTA cut off the public data feed he was using.
*   **Waymo:** In response to the mass-hail stunt that created a traffic jam of autonomous vehicles, a Waymo spokesperson stated that their systems are designed to manage such scenarios, which can occur naturally near large events, and that they were refining their system to handle distribution at specific locations. The company's response was measured, acknowledging the event while emphasizing system resilience.

**Professional Impact:**
The notoriety and technical skill demonstrated through his public projects have also had a positive impact on his career. A WIRED report noted that Walz was hired by OpenAI to research new ways for humans to interact with AI, indicating that his unconventional portfolio is highly valued within the tech industry.
